summary:
Alexander the Great's conquest of Egypt in 332 BC was one of the swiftest and most politically significant of his campaigns. After defeating the Persian forces at Issus (333 BC), Alexander marched south through the Levant, besieging Tyre and Gaza before entering Egypt, which surrendered without significant resistance — the Egyptians welcomed him as a liberator from Persian rule. The Persian satrap Mazaces handed over the country and its treasury.

Alexander's actions in Egypt were carefully calibrated for legitimacy: he traveled to the oracle of Amun at Siwa Oasis in the Libyan Desert, where the oracle reportedly confirmed his divine parentage as a son of Amun (equated with Zeus). He founded Alexandria on the Mediterranean coast in 331 BC — a city that would become the cultural and intellectual capital of the Hellenistic world under the Ptolemies. He was crowned pharaoh and styled himself the successor of Egyptian kings, wearing Egyptian regalia and making sacrifices to Egyptian gods. Egypt became the most stable and wealthy of the successor kingdoms after his death, its Ptolemaic rulers maintaining this dual Greek-Egyptian royal identity for nearly three centuries.
